---
title: "Functions, Loops, and Dictionaries"
subtitle: "Machine Learning in Finance · Session 2"
# Not self-contained: quarto-live's Pyodide runtime cannot resolve its worker
# from a data: URI, so embed-resources is off. Ship session_02.html together
# with session_02_files/ and SERVE OVER HTTP (double-click serve.cmd, or
# publish to a URL). The live code cells do not run from file://.
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../theme/mlfin.scss]
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    center: false
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    background-transition: none
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: false
    progress: true
    hash: true
    history: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    auto-stretch: false
engine: jupyter
pyodide:
  cell-options:
    autorun: true
    startover: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# One return is easy

Last session you stored prices in a **list**, indexed it, and computed a
**simple return** from two prices. That return is a single line:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
(prices[1] - prices[0]) / prices[0]
```

That is the return for **one** day, from the first close to the second.


# A year is 252 numbers

To study a stock you want **every** day's return. Apple traded about **252
days** in 2024, so that is 251 returns.

Writing them out one line at a time, `(prices[1] - prices[0]) / prices[0]`, then
`(prices[2] - prices[1]) / prices[1]`, and so on, is not realistic.

::: {.keypoint}
You need a way to say: do this once for every day. That is a **loop**, and it is
where we start today.
:::


# Today

By the end of this session you can:

- repeat work automatically with a **for loop** and a **while loop**
- make choices with **if**
- package a calculation into a **function** you write yourself
- organise results by name in a **dictionary**
- combine all of it to measure risk properly, as **volatility**

<br>

And you continue the course **case**: turning last session's rough risk gauge
into a real one.


# 1 · For loops {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 1
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Doing something once for every item in a list.
:::


# How a loop runs

Step through it one click at a time. Watch the highlighted line, and how the
variable and the output change:

```{=html}
<div class="sr">
<div class="sr-code">
<div class="sr-line">prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87]</div>
<div class="sr-line">for price in prices:</div>
<div class="sr-line">    print(price)</div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="1" style="--a:1; --b:1;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl-inline fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="1" style="--r:0; --c:10; --w:6;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="2" style="--a:2; --b:2;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl-inline fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="2" style="--r:0; --c:10; --w:6;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="3" style="--a:1; --b:1;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl-inline fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="3" style="--r:0; --c:18; --w:6;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="4" style="--a:2; --b:2;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl-inline fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="4" style="--r:0; --c:18; --w:6;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="5" style="--a:1; --b:1;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl-inline fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="5" style="--r:0; --c:26; --w:6;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="6" style="--a:2; --b:2;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl-inline fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="6" style="--r:0; --c:26; --w:6;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="7" style="--a:1; --b:1;"></div>
</div>
<div class="sr-state">
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="1"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">price = 183.56</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out"></div><div class="sr-note">take the first item</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="2"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">price = 183.56</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out">183.56</div><div class="sr-note">run the body</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="3"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">price = 182.19</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out">183.56</div><div class="sr-note">take the next item</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="4"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">price = 182.19</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out">183.56
182.19</div><div class="sr-note">run the body</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="5"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">price = 179.87</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out">183.56
182.19</div><div class="sr-note">take the next item</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="6"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">price = 179.87</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out">183.56
182.19
179.87</div><div class="sr-note">run the body</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="7"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">no items left</div><div class="sr-lbl">output</div><div class="sr-out">183.56
182.19
179.87</div><div class="sr-note">the list is finished, so the loop stops</div></div>
</div>
</div>
```


# A first loop

Now run it for real. Instead of printing prices one by one, let Python walk the
list:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87]
for price in prices:
    print(price)
```

`for price in prices:` reads as "for each price in the list". The indented line
runs once per item.


# The loop variable

Each time round, `price` holds the **next** value in the list:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87]
for price in prices:
    print("close:", price)
```

`price` is an ordinary variable, and you choose its name.


# Indentation marks the body

Python knows what is **inside** the loop from the indentation:

```python
for price in prices:
    print(price)     # indented: inside the loop, runs each time
print("done")        # not indented: runs once, after the loop
```

The colon `:` opens the loop; the indented lines below it are its body.


# Predict the output [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

Before running, decide how many lines this prints, and what they are.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
prices = [10, 20, 30, 40]
for p in prices:
    print(p * 2)
```

::: {.muted .small}
One line for each item, or something else? Press Run to check.
:::


# Adding up a list, by hand

A loop can build a running **total**. Start at zero, then add each price:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
total = 0
for price in prices:
    total = total + price
total
```

This is exactly what `sum(prices)` does. Now you can see inside it.


# The accumulator pattern

That shape comes up constantly:

- start a variable at a neutral value (`0` for a sum)
- update it once per item, inside the loop
- read it after the loop

::: {.keypoint}
Set up, then loop and update, then use the result. Summing, counting, and
averaging all follow this one pattern.
:::


# A shorthand: `+=`

Updating a variable from itself, `total = total + r`, is so common that Python
gives it a shorthand:

```{pyodide}
total = 0
for r in [0.01, -0.02, 0.03]:
    total += r        # same as: total = total + r
total
```

`total += r` means `total = total + r`. The same works for `-=`, `*=`, and
`/=`. We will use it for accumulators from here on.


# Looping a fixed number of times

`range(n)` gives the whole numbers `0, 1, ... , n - 1` to loop over:

```{pyodide}
for i in range(5):
    print(i)
```

Use it when you care about **positions**, not the values themselves.


# range with a start

`range(start, stop)` begins at `start` and stops **before** `stop`:

```{pyodide}
for i in range(1, 5):
    print(i)
```

Just like slicing, the stop value is not included.


# Reaching two days at once

To compare each day with the one before it, loop over positions and use `i` and
`i - 1`:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
for i in range(1, len(prices)):
    print(prices[i], "after", prices[i - 1])
```

Starting at `1` means the first pair is day 2 against day 1. There is no day
before day 1.


# Starting from an empty list

To collect results, begin with an **empty list** and grow it with `.append`:

```{pyodide}
returns = []            # empty, ready to fill
returns.append(0.012)
returns.append(-0.004)
returns
```

`[]` is a list with nothing in it yet. This is how you gather values as a loop
runs.


# Every daily return, at once

Put the two ideas together: loop over positions, and append each return:

```{pyodide}
prices = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
returns = []
for i in range(1, len(prices)):
    r = (prices[i] - prices[i - 1]) / prices[i - 1]
    returns.append(r)
returns
```

Five prices give four daily returns. The same formula as last session, now
applied to the whole list.


# Your turn [1]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Use a loop to add up all the returns into `total`. Start `total` at zero, and
add each return inside the loop.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
returns = [0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006]

total = ...
for r in returns:
    ...
total
```


# Your turn 1: answer

The accumulator pattern, on the returns:

```python
returns = [0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006]

total = 0
for r in returns:
    total += r
total
```

**Result: `0.011`.** Start at zero, add each return once, read the total after
the loop.


# 2 · Making choices {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 2
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Running code only when a condition is true.
:::


# The if statement

Last session a comparison gave `True` or `False`. **if** acts on that answer:

```{pyodide}
price = 260
if price > 250:
    print("above 250")
```

The indented block runs only when the condition is `True`. Change `price` to
`240` and run it again.


# else: the other case

`else` handles what happens when the condition is `False`:

```{pyodide}
price = 240
if price > 250:
    print("above 250")
else:
    print("250 or below")
```


# elif: more than two cases

`elif` (short for else-if) adds further cases, checked in order:

```{pyodide}
r = -0.02
if r > 0:
    print("up day")
elif r < 0:
    print("down day")
else:
    print("flat day")
```


# Predict the branch [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

What does this print? Decide before you run it.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
r = 0.0
if r > 0:
    print("up day")
elif r < 0:
    print("down day")
else:
    print("flat day")
```

::: {.muted .small}
Which branch is true when `r` is exactly zero?
:::


# if inside a loop

Put the two together: check a condition for every item. Count the up-days:

```{pyodide}
returns = [0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006]
up_days = 0
for r in returns:
    if r > 0:
        up_days += 1
up_days
```

The `if` sits inside the loop body, indented once more. `up_days` grows only on
positive days.


# Indentation stacks

Each `:` opens a block, indented one step further than the last:

```python
for r in returns:            # loop body: indented once
    if r > 0:                # if body: indented twice
        up_days += 1
```

Read the indentation to see what belongs to what.


# Your turn [2]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Count how many returns were **negative** (a down day).

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
returns = [0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006]

down_days = 0
for r in returns:
    ...
down_days
```


# Your turn 2: answer

The up-day count, with the comparison flipped:

```python
returns = [0.012, -0.004, 0.008, -0.011, 0.006]

down_days = 0
for r in returns:
    if r < 0:
        down_days += 1
down_days
```

**Result: `2`.** Two of the five returns are below zero.


# 3 · While loops {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 3
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Repeating for as long as a condition stays true.
:::


# When you do not know how many times

A **for loop** runs once per item. A **while loop** runs for as long as a
condition holds, however many times that takes.

How many years to grow 100 into 150 at 10% a year?

```{pyodide}
value = 100
years = 0
while value < 150:
    value *= 1.10
    years += 1
years
```

You did not have to know the answer in advance. The loop keeps going until
`value` reaches 150.


# Watching a while loop

Step through it. The condition is checked before each pass; watch `value` and
`years` change:

```{=html}
<div class="sr">
<div class="sr-code">
<div class="sr-line">value = 100</div>
<div class="sr-line">years = 0</div>
<div class="sr-line">while value &lt; 150:</div>
<div class="sr-line">    value *= 1.10</div>
<div class="sr-line">    years += 1</div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="1" style="--a:2; --b:2;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="2" style="--a:3; --b:4;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="3" style="--a:3; --b:4;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="4" style="--a:3; --b:4;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="5" style="--a:3; --b:4;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="6" style="--a:3; --b:4;"></div>
<div class="sr-hl fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="7" style="--a:2; --b:2;"></div>
</div>
<div class="sr-state">
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="1"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 100.00<br>years = 0</div><div class="sr-note">100 &lt; 150 ? yes, enter the loop</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="2"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 110.00<br>years = 1</div><div class="sr-note">grow value, count a year</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="3"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 121.00<br>years = 2</div><div class="sr-note">still below 150, go again</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="4"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 133.10<br>years = 3</div><div class="sr-note">still below 150, go again</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="5"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 146.41<br>years = 4</div><div class="sr-note">still below 150, go again</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="6"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 161.05<br>years = 5</div><div class="sr-note">grow value, count a year</div></div>
<div class="sr-panel fragment current-visible" data-fragment-index="7"><div class="sr-lbl">variables</div><div class="sr-mem">value = 161.05<br>years = 5</div><div class="sr-note">161.05 &lt; 150 ? no, the loop stops</div></div>
</div>
</div>
```


# Careful: it must be able to stop

Something inside the loop has to move the condition towards `False`, or it runs
forever:

```python
value = 100
while value < 150:
    years += 1     # value never changes, so this never stops
```

::: {.keypoint}
A `while` loop needs an exit. Make sure each pass changes something the
condition depends on. If a cell seems stuck, interrupt it with the stop button.
:::


# Your turn [3]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

How many years to grow **1000** into **1500** at **8%** a year? Complete the
while loop.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
value = 1000
years = 0
while value < 1500:
    ...
years
```

::: {.muted .small}
Two lines in the body: grow `value`, then count the year.
:::


# Your turn 3: answer

```python
value = 1000
years = 0
while value < 1500:
    value *= 1.08
    years += 1
years
```

**Result: `6`.** After five years the value is still under 1500; the sixth year
pushes it above.


# 4 · Your own functions {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 4
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Naming a calculation once, so you can reuse it anywhere.
:::


# You already use functions

`print`, `len`, `max`, and `sum` are functions: a name, then inputs in brackets.
Today you write your **own**.


# Defining a function

`def` creates a function. You give it a name, its inputs, and what it returns:

```{pyodide}
def simple_return(p0, p1):
    return (p1 - p0) / p0

simple_return(183.56, 182.19)
```

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="920" height="150" viewBox="0 0 920 150" role="img" aria-label="Two inputs p0 and p1 enter the function simple_return, which returns one value">
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">
    <rect x="40" y="30" width="120" height="42" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="40" y="82" width="120" height="42" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="100" y="57" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" fill="#141413">p0</text>
    <text x="100" y="109" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" fill="#141413">p1</text>
  </g>
  <line x1="160" y1="51" x2="330" y2="65" stroke="#52514e" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <line x1="160" y1="103" x2="330" y2="85" stroke="#52514e" stroke-width="1.6"/>
  <rect x="335" y="35" width="270" height="80" rx="8" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="470" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" fill="#0d366b" font-weight="600" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">simple_return</text>
  <text x="470" y="95" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" fill="#52514e" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">the calculation</text>
  <line x1="605" y1="75" x2="720" y2="75" stroke="#52514e" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="720,75 707,68 707,82" fill="#52514e"/>
  <rect x="725" y="52" width="150" height="46" rx="5" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="800" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#141413" font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">a return</text>
</svg>
:::


# The shape of a def

```python
def simple_return(p0, p1):     # name, and inputs (the parameters)
    return (p1 - p0) / p0      # the value it hands back
```

- `def name(parameters):` opens the function
- the indented body is what it does
- `return` hands a value back to whoever called it


# Call it as often as you like

Once defined, a function can be used again and again:

```{pyodide}
def simple_return(p0, p1):
    return (p1 - p0) / p0

print(simple_return(100, 110))
print(simple_return(50, 45))
```

Write the formula once, and trust it everywhere. There is no copy to get wrong.


# Parameters and arguments

The names in the `def` are **parameters**; the values you pass in are
**arguments**:

```python
def simple_return(p0, p1):   # p0 and p1 are parameters
    return (p1 - p0) / p0

simple_return(100, 110)      # 100 and 110 are arguments
```

Inside that call, `p0` is `100` and `p1` is `110`.


# return hands a value back

Without `return`, a function does its work but gives nothing back:

```{pyodide}
def show(p0, p1):
    print((p1 - p0) / p0)   # prints, but does not return

x = show(100, 110)
print(x)
```

`x` is `None`: `show` printed a value but did not `return` one. Use `return`
when you want to keep the result.


# Too few arguments [predict]{.chip .chip-predict}

`simple_return` needs two inputs. What happens if you give it only one? Predict,
then run.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
def simple_return(p0, p1):
    return (p1 - p0) / p0

simple_return(100)
```

::: {.muted .small}
Python will not guess the missing value.
:::


# Default arguments

A parameter can have a **default**, used when you do not pass it:

```{pyodide}
def grow(price, rate=0.05):
    return price * (1 + rate)

print(grow(100))         # uses the default rate of 5%
print(grow(100, 0.10))   # passes 10% instead
```

`rate=0.05` in the `def` sets the default. Passing a value overrides it.


# Documenting a function

A **docstring** is a short description in triple quotes, just under the `def`:

```{pyodide}
def simple_return(p0, p1):
    """Percentage change in price from p0 to p1."""
    return (p1 - p0) / p0

help(simple_return)
```

::: {.muted .small}
Your editor shows the docstring when you hover over the function, and
`help(...)` prints it. A one-line note now saves confusion later.
:::


# A function can contain a loop

Functions and loops combine. Here is the average of a list, as a function:

```{pyodide}
def average(values):
    total = 0
    for v in values:
        total += v
    return total / len(values)

average([10, 20, 30])
```

The name `average` now stands for four lines of work.


# Your turn [4]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Write a function `total_return(prices)` that returns the return from the first
price to the last.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
def total_return(prices):
    ...

total_return([100, 104, 103, 110])
```

::: {.muted .small}
The first price is `prices[0]`, the last is `prices[-1]`.
:::


# Your turn 4: answer

```python
def total_return(prices):
    return (prices[-1] - prices[0]) / prices[0]

total_return([100, 104, 103, 110])
```

**Result: `0.10`.** The function works for a list of any length, because
`prices[-1]` is always the last one.


# 5 · Dictionaries {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 5
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Storing values by name, instead of by position.
:::


# Positions, or names

A list finds a value by its **position**, `prices[0]`. A **dictionary** finds a
value by a **key** you choose, such as a stock's name.

::: {.diagram}
<svg width="740" height="180" viewBox="0 0 740 180" role="img" aria-label="A dictionary mapping the key AAPL to 0.36 and the key KO to 0.07">
  <g font-family="Cascadia Mono, monospace">
    <rect x="90" y="34" width="170" height="48" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="90" y="98" width="170" height="48" rx="6" fill="#eef4fc" stroke="#1c5cab" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="175" y="65" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" fill="#0d366b">"AAPL"</text>
    <text x="175" y="129" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" fill="#0d366b">"KO"</text>
    <text x="150" y="22" font-size="14" fill="#1c5cab" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">keys</text>
    <line x1="262" y1="58" x2="392" y2="58" stroke="#52514e" stroke-width="2"/>
    <polygon points="392,58 379,51 379,65" fill="#52514e"/>
    <line x1="262" y1="122" x2="392" y2="122" stroke="#52514e" stroke-width="2"/>
    <polygon points="392,122 379,115 379,129" fill="#52514e"/>
    <rect x="397" y="34" width="150" height="48" rx="6" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <rect x="397" y="98" width="150" height="48" rx="6" fill="#ffffff" stroke="#c3c2b7" stroke-width="1.5"/>
    <text x="472" y="65" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" fill="#141413">0.36</text>
    <text x="472" y="129" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" fill="#141413">0.07</text>
    <text x="452" y="22" font-size="14" fill="#898781" font-family="Segoe UI, sans-serif">values</text>
  </g>
</svg>
:::


# Making a dictionary

Curly braces, holding `key: value` pairs:

```{pyodide}
year_return = {"AAPL": 0.36, "KO": 0.07}
year_return
```

Here the keys are tickers, and the values are their yearly returns.


# Looking up a value

Use the key in square brackets, the way a position works for a list:

```{pyodide}
year_return = {"AAPL": 0.36, "KO": 0.07}
year_return["AAPL"]
```

A key like `"AAPL"` is usually clearer than a bare number such as `[0]`.


# Adding and updating

Assign to a new key to add a pair; assign to an existing key to change it:

```{pyodide}
year_return = {"AAPL": 0.36, "KO": 0.07}
year_return["NVDA"] = 1.80
year_return
```


# Looping over a dictionary

A `for` loop over a dictionary walks its **keys**:

```{pyodide}
year_return = {"AAPL": 0.36, "KO": 0.07}
for ticker in year_return:
    print(ticker, year_return[ticker])
```

`ticker` takes each key in turn, and `year_return[ticker]` reads its value.


# A dictionary can hold lists

Values can be anything, including whole price series:

```{pyodide}
prices = {
    "AAPL": [183.56, 182.19, 179.87],
    "KO": [59.20, 59.05, 59.40],
}
prices["KO"]
```

One object now holds every stock's prices, each under its own name.


# Your turn [5]{.chip .chip-turn} {.your-turn}

Add Coca-Cola's volatility of `0.011` to the dictionary, then read it back.

```{pyodide}
#| autorun: false
vol = {"AAPL": 0.020}

...
vol["KO"]
```


# Your turn 5: answer

```python
vol = {"AAPL": 0.020}

vol["KO"] = 0.011
vol["KO"]
```

**Result: `0.011`.** Assigning to a new key adds it; looking it up reads it
back.


# 6 · Measuring risk properly {.divider}

::: {.divider-kicker}
Part 6
:::

::: {.divider-note}
Everything from today, combined into a real measure of risk.
:::


# From range to volatility

Last session's risk gauge used only two days, the highest and the lowest. A
better measure uses **every** day's return. That measure is **volatility**: how
much the daily returns typically spread around their average.


# What volatility is

Volatility is the **standard deviation** of the daily returns:

$$ \sigma = \sqrt{\ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i} \left(r_i - \bar{r}\right)^2\ } $$

In words: take each return's distance from the average, square it, average those
squares, and take the square root. It is the typical size of a daily move.


# The calculation, in three steps

Each step is something you can already do:

1. the **daily returns**, with a loop (Part 1)
2. their **average**, `sum(returns) / len(returns)`
3. each return's **squared distance** from the average, averaged, then
   square-rooted with `** 0.5`

We now build these into one function, one step at a time.


# Build it up: the daily returns

Start the function with just step 1, the returns loop. It is not finished yet:

```python
def volatility(prices):
    returns = []
    for i in range(1, len(prices)):
        returns.append((prices[i] - prices[i - 1]) / prices[i - 1])
    # still to come: the average, and the spread
```

So far it only collects the daily returns into a list.


# Build it up: the average

Add step 2, the average return. Still not finished:

```python
def volatility(prices):
    returns = []
    for i in range(1, len(prices)):
        returns.append((prices[i] - prices[i - 1]) / prices[i - 1])
    mean = sum(returns) / len(returns)
    # still to come: the spread around the mean
```

Now it also has the average of those returns.


# Build it up: the volatility

Add step 3, the spread, and `return` it. Now it is complete, so run it:

```{pyodide}
def volatility(prices):
    returns = []
    for i in range(1, len(prices)):
        returns.append((prices[i] - prices[i - 1]) / prices[i - 1])
    mean = sum(returns) / len(returns)
    squared = 0
    for r in returns:
        squared += (r - mean) ** 2
    return (squared / len(returns)) ** 0.5

volatility([183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48])
```

Every idea from today is here: two loops, an empty list, `+=`, and a `return`.


# Apple's volatility

Now reuse the function. Give it Apple's week of prices:

```{pyodide}
aapl = [183.56, 182.19, 179.87, 179.15, 183.48]
volatility(aapl)
```

::: {.muted .small}
This calls the `volatility` function defined on the previous slide.
:::


# Coca-Cola's volatility

The same function, a different list:

```{pyodide}
ko = [59.20, 59.05, 59.40, 59.10, 59.25]
volatility(ko)
```

::: {.muted .small}
A smaller number than Apple's: Coca-Cola's price moved less from day to day.
:::


# Two stocks, one dictionary

Keep both answers together, each under its ticker:

```{pyodide}
vol = {"AAPL": volatility(aapl), "KO": volatility(ko)}
vol
```

::: {.muted .small}
Apple's number is the larger one. This is the volatility comparison the case
asks you to build.
:::


# The returns that volatility measures

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "axes.facecolor": "#fcfcfb",
    "savefig.facecolor": "#fcfcfb", "font.family": ["Segoe UI", "Arial", "sans-serif"],
    "font.size": 12, "text.color": "#141413", "axes.edgecolor": "#c3c2b7",
    "axes.labelcolor": "#52514e", "axes.titlecolor": "#141413", "axes.titlesize": 13,
    "axes.spines.top": False, "axes.spines.right": False, "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.color": "#e6e5df", "grid.linewidth": 0.8, "xtick.color": "#898781",
    "ytick.color": "#898781", "xtick.labelsize": 10.5, "ytick.labelsize": 10.5,
})

data_file = Path("../data/aapl_2024_closes.csv")
if not data_file.exists():
    raise FileNotFoundError(
        "Missing ../data/aapl_2024_closes.csv - clone the full course "
        "repository, or run tools/build_dataset.py from the repository root."
    )
aapl = pd.read_csv(data_file, parse_dates=["date"])
prices = aapl["close"].tolist()
dates = aapl["date"].tolist()
returns = [(prices[i] - prices[i - 1]) / prices[i - 1] for i in range(1, len(prices))]
rdates = dates[1:]
mean = sum(returns) / len(returns)
sigma = (sum((r - mean) ** 2 for r in returns) / len(returns)) ** 0.5

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.6, 3.3))
ax.axhspan(mean - sigma, mean + sigma, color="#dcebfd", alpha=0.9, zorder=0)
ax.axhline(mean, color="#898781", linewidth=1, zorder=1)
ax.plot(rdates, returns, color="#2a78d6", linewidth=1.0, zorder=2)
ax.set_ylim(-0.055, 0.055)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mtick.PercentFormatter(1.0))
ax.annotate("most days fall within this band:\nits width is the volatility",
            xy=(rdates[150], mean + sigma),
            xytext=(rdates[120], 0.033),
            fontsize=10.5, color="#52514e",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#898781", linewidth=1))
ax.set_title("Apple (AAPL) daily returns, 2024", loc="left")
ax.set_ylabel("daily return")
ax.margins(x=0.01)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Each point is one day's return. Volatility is the width of the band they mostly
fall inside.


# What stays with you {.smaller}

::: {.recap-grid}
::: {.recap-item}
**For loops**
`for x in items:` runs the body once per item.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**While loops**
`while condition:` repeats until the condition is false.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**if / elif / else**
Run a block only when a condition holds.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Functions**
`def name(args):` with `return`, plus defaults and a docstring.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Dictionaries**
`{key: value}`, looked up by key, not position.
:::
::: {.recap-item}
**Volatility**
The standard deviation of the daily returns.
:::
:::


# The course case, Part 2 [case]{.chip .chip-case}

The risk report continues. Last session you compared Apple and Coca-Cola with a
rough price range. This session you replace it with a proper measure:

::: {.keypoint}
Build a volatility calculation from the daily returns, run it on each stock, and
organise the results in a dictionary. Still no pandas: just today's loops and
functions.
:::


# Case Part 2: this week

`session_02_case.ipynb`, done at home before Session 3:

- reload Part 1's data and the findings you saved
- build the daily-return loop into a function of your own
- compute proper volatility for Apple and Coca-Cola, and compare it to the crude
  range from Part 1
- rank a few more stocks by risk, organised in a dictionary

::: {.muted .small}
Hints and full solutions are folded into the notebook. Work first, then check.
:::


# Before Session 3

<div class="pillars">
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Homework</div><div class="pillar-title">Exercises</div><div class="pillar-body"><code>session_02_exercises.ipynb</code>: loops, if, while, functions, and dictionaries. Hints and solutions folded in.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">Homework</div><div class="pillar-title">Case Part 2</div><div class="pillar-body"><code>session_02_case.ipynb</code>: the volatility of Apple versus Coca-Cola.</div></div>
<div class="pillar"><div class="pillar-tag">If stuck</div><div class="pillar-title">Get help</div><div class="pillar-body">Ask a friend, ask an AI for a hint, or email me at <code>jobo@econ.au.dk</code>. Stuck over 15 minutes? Note it and bring it next session.</div></div>
</div>

::: {.muted .small}
**Next session:** packages. pandas, numpy, and matplotlib do all of this on
eleven stocks at once, and plot the results.
:::
